text: Which of the following statements about Java serialization are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: A class must implement Serializable to be serialized.
Fields marked transient are not included in serialization.
static fields are serialized by default.
serialVersionUID is recommended to control versioning.
The no-arg constructor is called during deserialization.

explanation: static fields are NOT serialized (they belong to the class, not instance). The no-arg constructor is NOT called during deserialization — Java restores the object state from the byte stream directly.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 638–644

text: Which of the following create a valid Path object? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Path is an interface — you cannot use new Path(). Path.from() does not exist. Valid ways: Path.of(), Paths.get(), or FileSystems.getDefault().getPath().
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 612–615

text: What is the output of the following code, assuming the file does NOT exist?
explanation: Files.exists() returns false when the file does not exist. Files.notExists() returns true when the file does not exist. They are NOT strict negations of each other — both can return false if the file status cannot be determined (e.g., permission issues).
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 617–619

text: Which class provides methods to read, write, copy, and move files using Path objects?
explanation: java.nio.file.Files is a utility class with static methods to operate on files and directories via Path objects: Files.copy(), Files.move(), Files.readAllLines(), Files.write(), etc. Path represents a file location. File is the legacy java.io class.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 619–630

text: What is the correct way to read all lines from a file using NIO.2?
explanation: Files.readAllLines(Path) reads all lines into a List<String>. Files.lines(Path) returns a Stream<String> which must be collected. FileReader has no readLines() method. Path has no readLines() method.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 623–625

text: Which of the following about BufferedReader and BufferedWriter are correct? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: BufferedReader adds buffering to reduce underlying read calls.
BufferedReader.readLine() returns null at end of stream.
BufferedWriter.newLine() writes a platform-specific line separator.
BufferedReader can wrap any Reader, not just FileReader.

explanation: BufferedReader extends Reader (not InputStream) and adds a buffer plus readLine(). readLine() returns null at EOF. newLine() on BufferedWriter writes the system line.separator. BufferedReader can wrap any Reader implementation.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 600–607

text: What is the primary difference between InputStream/OutputStream and Reader/Writer?

options.text: InputStream works with bytes; Reader works with characters.
InputStream is faster; Reader is more accurate.
Reader supports random access; InputStream does not.

explanation: InputStream and OutputStream deal with raw bytes (0–255). Reader and Writer deal with characters (Unicode), handling character encoding internally. For text files, Reader/Writer is preferred; for binary data, InputStream/OutputStream is used.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 596–600

text: Which of the following correctly writes a String to a file using NIO.2?

options.text: Both A and B are correct.

explanation: Files.write(Path, byte[]) writes bytes. Files.writeString(Path, CharSequence) (introduced in Java 11) writes a String directly. Both are valid. Path itself has no write() method.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 625–627

text: What is the output of Path.of("/a/b/../c").normalize().toString() on a Unix system?
explanation: normalize() resolves . and .. components without accessing the file system. /a/b/.. goes up from b to a, then /c is appended, giving /a/c.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 615–617

text: Which of the following Files methods return a Stream and should be used in a try-with-resources? (Choose all that apply.)
explanation: Files.lines(), Files.list(), and Files.walk() all return streams that hold open file handles. They should be used in a try-with-resources to ensure the underlying stream is closed. Files.readAllLines() and Files.readAllBytes() read everything at once and close the file, so they do not need try-with-resources.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 627–632

text: What happens when you attempt to deserialize an object whose class has a different serialVersionUID than the serialized form?

options.text: The object is deserialized with default values for new fields.
The JVM ignores the mismatch and deserializes the object.

explanation: If the serialVersionUID of the class at deserialization time does not match the one in the serialized stream, Java throws InvalidClassException. This prevents accidentally deserializing data with incompatible class changes.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 641–643

text: Which method on Path returns the file name component without the parent path?
explanation: Path.getFileName() returns the last component of the path as a Path object. toString() on that gives 'file.txt'. Path.getName(int) returns the name element at a given index — not the same. toFile().getName() works but goes through java.io.File, which is the legacy API.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 612–617

text: What is the output of the following code?
explanation: relativize() computes the relative path from p1 to p2. Starting from /a/b/c, you go up two levels (.. to /a/b, .. to /a) and then down to d. Result: ../../d.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 615–617

text: Which of the following are differences between File and Path/Files? (Choose all that apply.)

options.text: Path is an interface; File is a class.
Files.copy() returns the destination Path; File.copy() does not exist.
File provides better exception messages than Path-based operations.
Path supports symbolic links; File has limited support.
Files methods throw IOException on errors; File methods mostly return boolean.

explanation: Path is an interface (NIO.2). File.copy() does not exist — copying required manual code. NIO.2 Path/Files handles symbolic links more explicitly. Files methods throw proper IOExceptions; File methods return booleans (making error diagnosis harder). NIO.2 provides better error messages, not File.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 610–614

text: What does the following code print?
explanation: Path.of("a", "b", "c") creates a relative path a/b/c. getNameCount() returns 3 (three components). getName(1) returns the element at index 1 (zero-based), which is 'b'.
source: Selikoff & Boyarsky, OCP Java SE 21 Developer Study Guide (Sybex, 2023), Chapter 14 – I/O, pp. 612–615
